title: Round to the Nearest Perfect Square

statement.markdown: Round a given number to the nearest perfect square. A perfect square is a number that can be expressed as the square of an integer. For example, 5 is rounded to 9 because 3^2 = 9 and 4^2 = 16. If the given number is a perfect square, return the number itself.

hints.text: If the number is a perfect square, return it.
Otherwise, find the two perfect squares around it.

editorial.title: Rounding to the Nearest Perfect Square
editorial.intuition: To find the nearest perfect square, first find the square root of the number. If it is an integer, then the number is a perfect square and can be returned as is.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach: Try every number below and above the input to see if they are perfect squares.
editorial.betterSolution: A better approach: Find the square root of the input number, then find the two perfect squares around it.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to find the integer part of the square root of the input number, then check if the square of the next integer is closer to the input number or not.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: Proof of correctness: Let the input number be n. Let sqrt_n be the square root of n. If sqrt_n is an integer, then n is a perfect square and can be returned as is. Otherwise, let p be the integer part of sqrt_n. The perfect square around n is p^2. The next perfect square is (p+1)^2. We compare n to p^2 and (p+1)^2 to determine which one is closer.
editorial.dryRun: Example run: Input number: 27. The square root of 27 is 5.196. The integer part is 5. The perfect squares around 27 are 25 and 36. 27 is closer to 36 than 25.
commonMistakes: Not checking if the number is a perfect square
Not comparing the two perfect squares around the input number
